verses.book: Jerome's Apology for Himself Against the Books of Rufinus.
verses.text: Let us understand what was the wrong done by my friend who, you say ‘falsified parts of your papers when they had not yet been corrected nor carried to completion, and it was the more possible to falsify them because very few if any as yet possessed them.’

verses.book: Jerome's Apology for Himself Against the Books of Rufinus.
verses.text: I have already said, and I now repeat, with protestations in the presence of God, that I did not approve his accusing you, nor of any Christian accusing another Christian; for what need is there that matters which can be corrected or set right in private should be published abroad to the stumbling and fall of many?

verses.book: Jerome's Apology for Himself Against the Books of Rufinus.
verses.text: But since each man lives for his own gullet, and a man does not by becoming your friend become master of your will, while I blame the accusing of a brother even when it is true, so also I cannot accept against a man of saintly character this accusation of falsifying your papers.

verses.book: Jerome's Apology for Himself Against the Books of Rufinus.
verses.text: How could a man who only knows Latin change anything in a translation from the Greek?

verses.book: Jerome's Apology for Himself Against the Books of Rufinus.
verses.text: Or how could he take out or put in anything in such books as the Περὶ ᾽Αρχῶν, in which everything is so closely knit together that one part hangs upon another, and anything that may be taken out or put in to suit your will must at once show out like a patch on a garment?

verses.book: Jerome's Apology for Himself Against the Books of Rufinus.
verses.text: What you ask me to do, it is for you to do yourself.

verses.book: Jerome's Apology for Himself Against the Books of Rufinus.
verses.text: Put on at least a small measure of natural if not of Christian modesty in your assertions; do not despise and trample upon your conscience, and imagine yourself justified by a show of words, when the facts are against you.

verses.book: Jerome's Apology for Himself Against the Books of Rufinus.
verses.text: If Eusebius bought your uncorrected papers for money in order to falsify them, produce the genuine papers which have not been falsified: and if you can shew that there is nothing heretical in them, he will become amenable to the charge of forgery.

verses.book: Jerome's Apology for Himself Against the Books of Rufinus.
verses.text: But, however much you may alter or correct them, you will not make them out to be catholic.

verses.book: Jerome's Apology for Himself Against the Books of Rufinus.
verses.text: If the error existed only in the words or in some few statements, what is bad might be cut off and what is good be substituted for it.

verses.book: Jerome's Apology for Himself Against the Books of Rufinus.
verses.text: But, when the whole discussion proceeds on a single principle, namely, the notion that the whole universe of reasonable creatures have fallen by their own will, and will hereafter return to a condition of unity: and that again from that starting point another fall will begin: what is there that you can amend, unless you alter the whole book?

verses.book: Jerome's Apology for Himself Against the Books of Rufinus.
verses.text: But if you were to think of doing this, you would no longer be translating another man’s work but composing a work of your own.

verses.book: Jerome's Apology for Himself Against the Books of Rufinus.
verses.text: However, I hardly see which way your argument tends.

verses.book: Jerome's Apology for Himself Against the Books of Rufinus.
verses.text: I suppose you mean that the papers being uncorrected and not having undergone a final revising were more easily falsified by Eusebius.

verses.book: Jerome's Apology for Himself Against the Books of Rufinus.
verses.text: Perhaps I am stupid; but the argument appears to me somewhat foolish and pointless.

verses.book: Jerome's Apology for Himself Against the Books of Rufinus.
verses.text: If the papers were uncorrected and had not undergone their final revision, the errors in them must be imputed not to Eusebius but to your sloth and delay in putting off their correction; and all the blame that can be laid upon him is that he circulated among the body of Christians writings which you had intended in course of time to correct.

verses.book: Jerome's Apology for Himself Against the Books of Rufinus.
verses.text: But if, as you assert, Eusebius falsified them, why do you put forward the allegation that they were uncorrected, and that they had gone out before the public without their final revision?

verses.book: Jerome's Apology for Himself Against the Books of Rufinus.
verses.text: For papers whether corrected or uncorrected are equally susceptible of falsification.

verses.book: Jerome's Apology for Himself Against the Books of Rufinus.
verses.text: But, No one, you say possessed these books, or very few.

verses.book: Jerome's Apology for Himself Against the Books of Rufinus.
verses.text: What contradictions this single sentence exhibits!

verses.book: Jerome's Apology for Himself Against the Books of Rufinus.
verses.text: If no one had these books, how could they be in the hands of a few?

verses.book: Jerome's Apology for Himself Against the Books of Rufinus.
verses.text: If a few possessed them, why do you state falsely that there were none?

verses.book: Jerome's Apology for Himself Against the Books of Rufinus.
verses.text: Then, when you say that a few had them, and by your own confession the statement that no one had them is overthrown, what becomes of your complaint that your secretary was bribed with money?

verses.book: Jerome's Apology for Himself Against the Books of Rufinus.
verses.text: Tell us the secretary’s name, the amount of the bribe, the place, the intermediary, the recipient.

verses.book: Jerome's Apology for Himself Against the Books of Rufinus.
verses.text: Of course the traitor has been cast off from you, and one convicted of so great a crime has been separated from all familiarity with you.

verses.book: Jerome's Apology for Himself Against the Books of Rufinus.
verses.text: Is it not more likely to be true that the copies of the work which Eusebius obtained were given him by those few friends whom you speak of, especially since these copies agree and coincide with one another so completely that there is not the difference of a single stroke.

verses.book: Jerome's Apology for Himself Against the Books of Rufinus.
verses.text: We might ask also whether it was quite wise to give a copy to others which you had not yet corrected?

verses.book: Jerome's Apology for Himself Against the Books of Rufinus.
verses.text: The documents had not received their last corrections, and yet other men possessed these errors of yours which needed correction.

verses.book: Jerome's Apology for Himself Against the Books of Rufinus.
verses.text: Do you not see that your falsehood will not hold together?

verses.book: Jerome's Apology for Himself Against the Books of Rufinus.
verses.text: Besides, what profit was there for you, at that particular moment—how would it have helped you in escaping from the condemnation of the bishops—that the book which was the subject of discussion should be open to everyone, and that you should thus be refuted by your own words?

verses.book: Jerome's Apology for Himself Against the Books of Rufinus.
verses.text: From all this it is clear, according to the epigram of the famous orator, that you have a good will for a lie, but not the art of framing it.
